    Jason A. Yoder

    Assistant Professor of CSSE at Rose-Hulman Institute of Technology. Researching bio-inspired AI, neuroevolution, and evolutionary development.

    • GitHub
    • Google Scholar
    • Email

    Teaching

    On this page

    • Courses at Rose-Hulman
      • CSSE220: Object-Oriented Software Development
      • CSSE490/CSSE314: Bio-Inspired Artificial Intelligence
      • CSSE280: Introduction to Web Programming
      • Other Courses
    • Prior Teaching

    “Teaching is the highest form of understanding.” – Aristotle

    My goal as a teacher is to understand each student’s motivations and challenges, and to create environments where students take on optimal challenges. I’m strongly influenced by embodied and embedded cognition: I see students as agents navigating complex environments, and my job is to structure those environments so that productive struggle leads to real understanding.

    I value learning quality over strict timeliness, provide generous extensions, and create explicit expectations to increase transparency. I use systems like “awesomesauce” extra credit and incentive points to recognize exceptional effort and engagement.

    Courses at Rose-Hulman

    CSSE220: Object-Oriented Software Development

    My most-taught course—17 offerings across 26 sections from Fall 2017 to Spring 2024. CSSE220 is the second course in the CSSE major sequence, bridging from Python to Java.

    I’ve invested heavily in this course, developing:

    • The Genetic Algorithm Research Project (GARP) as an alternative final project connecting coursework to real research
    • Exam wrappers for metacognitive reflection
    • Video lectures, pre/post-course surveys, and an incentive points system
    • TA-led course improvement projects (topic summaries, GradeScope migration, and more)

    CSSE490/CSSE314: Bio-Inspired Artificial Intelligence

    Designed from scratch and taught four times (2021–2023), this course was approved as a permanent offering (CSSE314) starting 2024–25. Based on Floreano & Mattiussi’s Bio-Inspired AI (MIT Press, 2008), it features:

    • “Build Your Own Homework” assignments
    • Jigsaw readings and Hypothesis annotations
    • Browser-based interactive labs
    • Final presentations complete with cookies, theatrical intros, intermissions, guest experts, and awards

    BioAI Course Schedule

    CSSE280: Introduction to Web Programming

    Taught four times (2019–2021) in a partially flipped format with “Follow Along” coding videos, integrated ZyBooks, and Airtable.

    Sample Student Portfolio

    Other Courses

    • CSSE340: Introduction to Cybersecurity (Winter 2021)
    • CSSE490: Evolutionary Computation (Fall 2019, 6 students, entirely new)
    • CSSE290: What is Life? (in Computers) – Co-developed and co-taught with Robert Williamson (Winter 2021). Led to a Learning & Teaching grant for a new Artificial Life course.
    • CSSE290: Research Lab I, II, III (2023–2024) – Organized the first-ever CSSE Research Symposium (Spring 2024) with a keynote speaker, alumni speakers, a printed program, and surprise awards.

    Prior Teaching

    Indiana University (2009–2017): Associate Instructor for 16 courses including Object-Oriented Software Development, Intro to Software Systems (6 semesters), Theory of Computation, and more.

    Johns Hopkins Center for Talented Youth (Summers 2011–2016): Taught Fundamentals of Computer Science to 7th–10th graders in intensive 3-week sessions (7 hours/day, 5 days/week). Won the Sarah D. Barder Fellowship Award in 2017.

    World Vision, Rural Peru (July 2007): Volunteer math instructor for ~120 children ages 5–12, taught in Spanish.
